name: The Definite Integral: Area Under the Curve
description:
In calculus, the definite integral of a function represents the signed area between the graph of the function and the x-axis. This visualization demonstrates how we approximate this area using Riemann Sums.

  • The Curve: Represents the function f(x) = 0.2x³ - x² + x + 4.
  • Riemann Rectangles (Blue): We divide the interval [0, 5] into discrete sub-intervals of width Δx. By calculating the area of these rectangles (height × width) and summing them up, we get an approximation of the total area.
  • The Integral (Green Shading): As the width of these rectangles Δx approaches zero (and the number of rectangles approaches infinity), the sum converges to the exact area under the curve.

This fundamental concept links the discrete (summation) with the continuous (integration), as defined by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
